content:
# Romans Chapter 7 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_007_page_001.jpg

1. Or are you ignorant, brothers — for I speak to [those] knowing [the] law — that the law has lordship over the human for as long a time as he lives?

2. For the married woman has been bound by law to the living husband; but if the husband dies, she has been released from the law of the husband.

3. So then, while the husband lives, she will be called an adulteress if she becomes [joined] to another man; but if the husband dies, she is free from the law, so that she is not an adulteress [though] becoming [joined] to another man.

4. So then, the brothers of me, you also were made dead to the law through the body of the Christos, so that you might become [joined] to another — to the [one] having been raised from [the] dead — that we might bear fruit to the God.

5. For when we were in the flesh, the passions of the sins, the [ones] through the law, were working in the members of us to bear fruit to the death.

6. But now we have been released from the law, having died [to that] in which we were held, so that we serve in newness of spirit and not in oldness of letter.

7. What then shall we say? [Is] the law sin? May it never be! But I did not know the sin except through [the] law. For I would not have known the coveting if the law did not say, "You shall not covet."

8. But the sin, having taken opportunity through the commandment, produced in me every [kind of] coveting. For apart from [the] law sin [is] dead.

9. And I was living apart from [the] law once; but when the commandment came, the sin came alive,

10. and I died. And the commandment, the [one] for life, this was found to me [to be] for death.

11. For the sin, having taken opportunity through the commandment, deceived me and through it killed [me].

12. So then the law [is] holy, and the commandment [is] holy and righteous and good.

13. Did therefore the good [thing] become death to me? May it never be! But the sin, that it might be shown [to be] sin, through the good [thing] was producing death to me, that the sin might become exceedingly sinful through the commandment.

14. For we know that the law is spiritual; but I am fleshly, having been sold under the sin.

15. For what I am working out, I do not understand. For what I wish, this I do not practice; but what I hate, this I do.

16. But if what I do not wish, this I do, I agree with the law that [it is] good.

17. But now [it is] no longer I working it out, but the sin dwelling in me.

18. For I know that there does not dwell in me — that is, in the flesh of me — [any] good. For the willing is present to me, but the working out of the good [is] not.

19. For the good which I wish, I do not do; but the evil which I do not wish, this I practice.

20. But if what I do not wish, this I do, [it is] no longer I working it out, but the sin dwelling in me.

21. I find then the law: that to me, the [one] wishing to do the good, that the evil is present to me.

22. For I delight in the law of the God according to the inner human,

23. but I see another law in the members of me warring against the law of the mind of me and taking me captive to the law of the sin, the [one] being in the members of me.

24. Wretched human [that] I [am]! Who will deliver me from the body of this death?

25. Thanks [be] to the God through Yeshua Christos the Lord of us! So then, I myself with the mind serve [the] law of God, but with the flesh [the] law of sin.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.7 — "You shall not covet" (οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις):** The tenth commandment (Exodus 20:17). Paul chooses this commandment because it is the most internal — coveting is a desire of the heart, not an external act. The law reaches into the inner life, and that is where sin is exposed.

2. **vv.7-13 — The "I":** Whether Paul speaks autobiographically, as Adam, or as representative humanity is debated. The aorist tenses ("I was living," "the commandment came") suggest a narrative — possibly the story of Adam's fall retold through Israel's encounter with Sinai.

3. **vv.14-25 — Present tense shift:** The shift to present tense ("I do not do what I want") is the key interpretive question. Is Paul describing the unregenerate person under law (Augustine early, most Reformers)? Or the regenerate Christian's ongoing struggle (Augustine later, Luther, Calvin)? Or a rhetorical persona? The intensity of the language favors existential experience.

4. **v.24 — "Wretched human that I am!":** ταλαίπωρος ἐγὼ ἄνθρωπος. The emotional climax of the chapter. Whether pre-conversion or post-conversion, the cry is genuine. The answer comes immediately in v.25 and is expanded in chapter 8.

5. **v.25 — Sinaiticus reading:** Sinaiticus reads χάρις τῷ θεῷ ("thanks to God") rather than the longer εὐχαριστῶ τῷ θεῷ ("I give thanks to God") found in some manuscripts.

— EMET, AI Translator
